_schema: Notable solar active regions with documented impact on geomagnetic activity. Each entry cites peer-reviewed literature or official agency reports. Modern NOAA region numbering began in 1972 (incrementing indefinitely); for events before that, entries use the USAF McMath-Hulbert catalog numbering with `numbering_scheme` set accordingly. This is a curated catalog, not a complete ingestion of the SWPC Solar Region Summary archive; see `scripts/sources/swpc-regions.mjs` for the bulk-ingestion stub planned for v1.x. Curator: HelioChronicles. Last reviewed 2026-04.

_fields.noaa_number: NOAA SWPC-assigned region number (integer); null for pre-1972 entries
_fields.pre_noaa_number: USAF McMath-Hulbert number for pre-1972 entries; null otherwise
_fields.first_observed: ISO date region first catalogued on the Earth-facing disk
_fields.last_observed: ISO date region last seen before limb crossing or decay
_fields.peak_magnetic_class: Mt. Wilson / Hale magnetic classification at peak complexity (e.g. beta-gamma-delta)
_fields.peak_mcintosh: Modified-Zurich (McIntosh) morphological class at peak (e.g. Fkc)
_fields.peak_area_msh: peak sunspot area in millionths of the solar hemisphere (MSH)
_fields.peak_flare: largest flare produced by this region (X-ray class)
_fields.flare_count_x_class: count of X-class flares produced (when known)
_fields.produced_events: list of ids in historical_storms.json this region drove

regions.significance: Produced the August 1972 ultra-fast CME (14.6 hour Sun-to-Earth transit, still the fastest on record). Would have delivered a fatal radiation dose to Apollo astronauts if timed between missions 16 and 17 — the canonical case study for crewed-spaceflight radiation risk.

sources.citation: Knipp, D. J. et al. (2018). On the Little-Known Consequences of the 4 August 1972 Ultra-Fast Coronal Mass Ejecta. Space Weather 16, 1635–1643.
Dodson, H. W. & Hedeman, E. R. (1972). McMath-Hulbert Observatory records of region 11976, 1972 July–August.

regions.significance: One of the most productive active regions of the 20th century. Produced the March 13-14 1989 CME that collapsed the Hydro-Québec grid — the modern canonical case for power-grid vulnerability to space weather.

sources.citation: Boteler, D. H. (2019). A 21st Century View of the March 1989 Magnetic Storm. Space Weather 17, 1427–1441.
NOAA SWPC Solar Region Summary archive, March 1989.
Allen, J. et al. (1989). Effects of the March 1989 solar activity. Eos Trans. AGU 70, 1479.

regions.significance: Drove the Bastille Day event — the first fully space-instrumented major storm, establishing the modern CME-timing template for operational forecasting.

sources.citation: Smith, Z. et al. (2003). A comparison of the Bastille Day event (2000 July 14) with the Halloween storm (2003 October 29). Solar Phys. 225, 377.
NOAA SWPC Solar Region Summary archive, July 2000.

regions.significance: Early contributor to the Halloween sequence. Rotated into view ahead of AR 10486 and set the scene for the cluster of October–November 2003 storms.

sources.citation: Gopalswamy, N. et al. (2005). Solar sources and geospace consequences of intense geomagnetic storms in October–November 2003. J. Geophys. Res. 110, A09S15.
NOAA SWPC Solar Region Summary archive, October 2003.

regions.significance: Primary driver of the Halloween storms. Produced the X17.2 flare of Oct 28, the X10.0 of Oct 29, and the X28+ of Nov 4 — the largest flare ever directly observed, which saturated GOES detectors. One of the largest active regions of cycle 23.

sources.citation: Gopalswamy, N. et al. (2005). Solar sources and geospace consequences of intense geomagnetic storms in October–November 2003. J. Geophys. Res. 110, A09S15.
Thomson, N. R. et al. (2004). Large solar flares and their ionospheric D region enhancements. J. Geophys. Res. 109, A06306.
NOAA SWPC Halloween 2003 event review.

regions.significance: Late contributor to the Halloween sequence. Though it produced no X-class flares itself, its M-class series sustained geomagnetic disturbance through early November and drove aurora visible to tropical latitudes.

sources.citation: Gopalswamy, N. et al. (2005). Solar sources and geospace consequences of intense geomagnetic storms in October–November 2003. J. Geophys. Res. 110, A09S15.

regions.significance: Most flare-productive active region of cycle 23 — nine X-class flares in 12 days, including X17.0 (Sept 7), X6.2 (Sept 8), and X6.2 (Sept 13). Drove the September 2005 G4 storm sequence.

sources.citation: NOAA SWPC Solar Region Summary and event archive, September 2005.
Wang, R. et al. (2006). Comparison of observed and modeled coronal magnetic structure for NOAA AR 10808. ApJ 652, L161.

regions.significance: LARGEST active region observed since November 1990 (AR 6368). Produced six X-class flares — yet remarkably few geoeffective CMEs, defying the expectation that area and eruptivity scale together. The canonical 'silent giant' case study.

sources.citation: Sun, X. et al. (2015). Why is the great solar active region 12192 flare-rich but CME-poor? ApJ Letters 804, L28.
Chen, H. et al. (2017). Why was the flare-rich AR 12192 CME-poor? ApJ 851, 133.

regions.significance: Produced the largest flare of cycle 24 (X9.3, Sept 6) and an X8.2 limb flare on Sept 10 that generated a widely-studied CME. Drove the Sept 7-8 G4 storm and disrupted HF radio during Atlantic hurricane response operations.

sources.citation: Redmon, R. J. et al. (2018). September 2017's geoeffective space weather and impacts to Caribbean radio communications during hurricane response. Space Weather 16, 1190–1201.
Sharykin, I. N. & Kosovichev, A. G. (2018). Dynamics of electric currents, magnetic field topology, and helioseismic response of a solar flare in NOAA AR 12673. ApJ 864, 86.

regions.significance: Largest active region of cycle 25 through 2024. Produced a week-long X-class flare series (May 8-11) and multiple Earth-directed CMEs that merged in transit to drive the Gannon (Mother's Day) G5 storm — first G5 since Halloween 2003.

sources.citation: NOAA SWPC (2024). Mother's Day Geomagnetic Storm event report.
Parker, W. E. & Linares, R. (2024). Satellite Drag Analysis During the May 2024 Gannon Geomagnetic Storm. AIAA preprint.
Hayakawa, H. et al. (2025). The solar and interplanetary context of the May 2024 extreme geomagnetic storm. ApJ Letters (in press).

regions.significance: Drove the October 2024 G4 storm — the second major SC25 storm within five months of Gannon, confirming that SC25 is producing extreme events at a cadence closer to cycle 23 than cycle 24.

sources.citation: NOAA SWPC (2024). October 2024 G4 storm event summary.
